title: From an observation to a testable question

objectives.statement: Distinguish a checkable observation from an explanation or interpretation in a short scientific case.
Convert an observation into a bounded empirical question that names a comparison or case, an observable record, and relevant conditions.
Judge whether evidence could answer a question in more than one direction and revise questions that assume a cause or lack measurable terms.

scenes.title: Notice before you explain
scenes.contentHtml:
Notice before you explain

Picture two freshly cut apple slices on a plate. One was brushed with lemon juice; the other was left alone. Twenty minutes later, the plain slice looks browner.

That small difference can start a real investigation. But first, pause over these two sentences:

  1. After twenty minutes, the untreated surface was darker than the lemon-coated surface.
  2. Lemon juice slowed the reaction that makes apples brown.

Which sentence records what someone noticed, and which one tries to explain it?

Choose before reading on.

The first sentence is an observation: it records something that could be checked, including what was compared and when. The second is an explanation. It may be right, but the color difference alone does not establish the cause.

That separation matters everywhere in science. A shape seen through a microscope, a temperature reading, and a pattern in a health dataset can all be observations. The story you tell about why the pattern appeared is an inference or explanation. Science needs both, but it does not treat them as the same thing.

In this lesson, you will learn how to move from “I noticed something” to a question that evidence can answer. By the end, you should be able to:

  • separate a record from the explanation attached to it;
  • name what you would compare or examine and what you would record;
  • set enough boundaries that another person can understand the question; and
  • leave room for the evidence to surprise you.

You do not need prior laboratory experience. Careful everyday noticing is enough to begin.

This lesson stops at the question. The next lessons take up hypotheses, predictions, variables, controls, measurement quality, and study design. There is no single script called “the scientific method” that every field follows in the same order; today you are learning one move that appears across many kinds of science.

Source Note
claims claim-observation-explanation, claim-empirical-question, claim-multiple-scientific-methodssources source-nrc-framework

The National Research Council distinguishes observations and data from the explanations built from them, describes asking empirically answerable questions as a core scientific practice, and cautions against reducing science to one fixed method.

scenes.title: Build a question you could answer
scenes.contentHtml:
Build a question you could answer

“Why did the lemon stop the apple going bad?” sounds scientific, but it moves too fast. It assumes the lemon stopped something, calls browning “going bad,” and does not say what evidence would count as an answer.

A useful question builder has four parts:

  1. The case or contrast: What are you examining or comparing?
  2. The record: What could you observe or measure?
  3. The boundary: In which sample, setting, and time window?
  4. An open outcome: Could the record reasonably point in more than one direction?

The four parts are a drafting aid, not a magic sentence pattern. Some fields compare groups, some follow one system through time, and some use existing observations rather than changing anything. What they share is a question connected to possible evidence.

Worked Example
id worked-example-apple-questionlabel From a brown apple to an answerable question

Starting point. The untreated apple surface looked darker than the lemon-coated surface after twenty minutes.

What are we asking? We are not yet asking for the chemical mechanism. We first want to know whether the visible difference holds under a stated comparison.

Choose the contrast. Compare slices coated with lemon juice with slices coated with water. Naming both sides is clearer than saying “with lemon” and leaving the alternative unstated.

Choose the record. Record the number of minutes each slice takes to reach level 3 on a supplied browning card that runs from 0 to 5. This turns “looks bad” into something people can record in the same form. Whether that card is a good measure is a later measurement question; here it makes the proposed record visible.

Set the boundary. Limit the question to freshly cut Granny Smith apple slices kept between 22 and 24 degrees Celsius during the first 60 minutes. This does not guarantee a fair study. It tells us what the question is and is not about.

Write the question. For freshly cut Granny Smith apple slices kept at 22–24 °C, what is the difference in time to reach browning level 3 between lemon-juice-coated and water-coated slices during the first 60 minutes?

Check it. We could record a longer time, the same time, a shorter time, or no slice reaching level 3. Each result would tell us something. The wording does not force the answer to favor lemon.

Interpret the limit. Answering this question would describe the comparison under these conditions. It would not, by itself, explain the chemistry, prove that the same result holds for every apple, or show that browned food is unsafe.

Self-explanation. Before reading on, name the one change that most improved the original question and explain why.

A strong answer may choose the record: “going bad” became minutes to a stated browning level, so an observation can answer the question. Choosing the contrast and boundary also matters. If you only changed the grammar, try again and name how the possible evidence became clearer.

Testable, measurable, and falsifiable

People sometimes call a question “falsifiable.” More precisely, a question is empirically answerable; a hypothesis or claim is what evidence can count against.

For example, “lemon-coated slices take longer to brown” is a claim. Equal or shorter times would count against it under the stated conditions. The question above stays open: it asks what the difference is.

Use a practical test before you keep a question:

  • Can you name a record that would bear on the answer?
  • Could you describe a result that differs from your expectation?
  • Is the question narrow enough to investigate with the time, access, and tools available?
  • Does the wording avoid smuggling in a cause, judgment, or desired result?

Not every meaningful question is an empirical science question. “Is it fair to use animals in this study?” is an ethical question. Evidence can inform it, but evidence alone does not decide the value judgment. “How should a clinic spend a limited budget?” also needs priorities, not only measurements. Calling those questions non-empirical does not make them unimportant.

Source Note
claims claim-observation-explanation, claim-empirical-question, claim-bounded-question, claim-question-claim-boundary, claim-description-not-causationsources source-nrc-framework

The source supports the distinction between observations and explanations, the need for empirically answerable questions, and the link from a question to records or measurements within available scope. The four-part builder and apple example are original instructional syntheses.

scenes.title: Practise the turn from noticing to testing
scenes.contentHtml:
Practise the turn from noticing to testing

First, retrieve the question builder without looking back. Write four short prompts for yourself.

Check
id check-retrieve-builder

What four choices turn a broad curiosity into a question connected to possible evidence? Commit your list, then continue.

Compare your list with: case or contrast; observable record; boundary; open outcome. Equivalent wording is fine. If one is missing, add it and say what problem that part prevents.

One part supplied

Observation: in the same kitchen, a spoonful of sugar seemed to disappear faster in warm water than in cold water.

Complete this question:

For ___, what is the difference in ___ between ___ and ___ during ___?

One defensible completion is:

For 5 grams of granulated sugar stirred at the same rate in 200 millilitres of water, what is the difference in seconds until no crystals are visible between water at 15 °C and water at 45 °C during a single mixing trial?

This version names a contrast, a record, and conditions. It is still only a question, not a complete study plan. If your answer said “Why does heat make sugar dissolve faster?”, remove the assumed direction and make the record visible.

Less support

Observation: on three mornings after rain, the water in a campus pond looked cloudier than it did on three dry mornings.

Draft a question without using the words better, worse, or because. Then check whether your question identifies:

  • the pond or sampling locations;
  • rainy and dry periods, or one clearly defined time series;
  • a record such as turbidity measured in the same units; and
  • a time boundary.

A defensible example is: At the north and south sampling points of Campus Pond during September, how does turbidity measured at 09:00 differ on mornings after at least 10 millimetres of rain and mornings after 48 rain-free hours?

That question asks about a measured pattern. It does not yet establish that rainfall caused the difference.

Misconception
id misconception-cause-makes-testable

Commit first. Is “Does music cause better health?” testable as written? Answer yes or no and give one reason.

Tempting shortcut: adding “does X cause Y?” makes any question scientific.

Why the shortcut fails: music, better health, the people or system, and the time period are all unspecified. The word cause also asks for a stronger conclusion than a simple pattern can provide.

Repair: first decide what can be recorded and in whom or what. A narrower question might ask, “Among adult volunteers in a specified study, how does self-reported anxiety on a named scale differ immediately after ten minutes of silence and ten minutes of instrumental music?” Whether the study can support a causal conclusion depends on its design; later lessons handle that decision.

Recheck: revise “Does screen time cause poor sleep?” into a bounded question about a recordable pattern without claiming that one factor causes the other.

One possible recheck is: Among first-year university students who choose to join a seven-day diary study, how is reported screen use during the hour before bed associated with sleep duration recorded that night? This is answerable as an observational question. It does not show that screen use caused the sleep pattern, and it does not request private health data from you.

Independent practice

Choose an observation from biology, chemistry, physics, or everyday life. Write one sentence that records the observation without explaining it. Then write an empirical question with all four parts. Finally, write one possible result that would surprise you.

Check your work:

  • If the observation contains because, a hidden mechanism, or a judgment, rewrite it as a record.
  • If two people could collect completely different kinds of evidence and both claim to have answered the question, name the record more clearly.
  • If only your expected answer seems possible, remove the loaded wording.
  • If the question tries to settle a mechanism, a population, and a treatment decision at once, narrow it to one answerable step.
Source Note
claims claim-observation-explanation, claim-empirical-question, claim-bounded-question, claim-question-claim-boundary, claim-description-not-causationsources source-nrc-framework

The source describes questions that arise from observed features and patterns, questions that can be investigated empirically, and investigation planning that identifies records, measurements, and scope. All practice cases and values here are original teaching examples.

scenes.title: Carry the skill into a new setting
scenes.contentHtml:
Carry the skill into a new setting

Here is a fictional, aggregated campus-health record. It is designed for this lesson and contains no real patient information.

In a 2025 summary, 76% of scheduled follow-up visits were completed by patients recorded as living less than 10 kilometres from a campus clinic. The completion figure was 58% for patients recorded as living 10 kilometres or farther away.

Your task is not to explain the difference. Turn the observation into one bounded empirical question.

Before continuing, write a question that names:

  • the patients and clinic covered by the record;
  • the distance contrast;
  • the follow-up record;
  • the time period; and
  • wording that does not assume distance caused the difference.

One defensible question is:

Among patients with scheduled follow-up visits at the campus clinic in 2025, how did the proportion of completed visits differ between those recorded as living less than 10 kilometres away and those recorded as living 10 kilometres or farther away?

This question could be answered from the stated record. It does not tell us why the proportions differ. Transport access, appointment type, work schedules, recording errors, and many other features could matter; deciding among them requires new questions and suitable designs.

Now transfer once more: write a second question that would help narrow one possible explanation without asking for anyone's identity or personal medical details. For example, you could ask about the same completion measure across public-transport access categories in an appropriately authorized, de-identified dataset. Keep the wording descriptive unless the design can support a causal claim.

What to carry forward

From memory, complete this chain:

Observation → ___ → ___ → ___ → empirical question

A useful reconstruction is:

Observation → case or contrast → observable record → boundary and open outcome → empirical question

The next lesson will begin with a question like yours and ask what explanation and prediction could be tested. Later lessons will revisit the same question when you choose variables, measurements, and a study design.

Mastery check

The assessment contains three untimed items. Attempt each before opening its answer and reasoning. A complete response earns all ten available points: two for separating observation from explanation, four for constructing a bounded question, and four for evaluating a question in a new setting. Retakes are unlimited. On a retry, use the alternate cases in the answer feedback rather than copying your first response.

Accessibility and alternatives

Every task can be completed in prose, bullets, or a table. You may type, dictate into your own device, or use assistive technology; no audio upload is required. The lesson does not depend on color, an image, dragging, speed, physical apple preparation, or access to a laboratory. The apple and clinic cases are fully described in text. You never need to provide personal or medical information.

Recovery route

If observation and explanation blur together, circle words that name an unseen cause, judgment, or mechanism. Move those words out of the observation. Keep only what was recorded, where, and when.

If a question remains vague, write four headings—case or contrast, record, boundary, open outcome—and fill one line under each before trying the sentence again.

If you have a meaningful question that evidence alone cannot settle, do not force it into scientific wording. State the empirical part separately from the ethical, practical, or value decision.

If causal wording keeps slipping in, begin with “How does ___ differ between ___?” or “What pattern links ___ and ___?” That produces a descriptive question. Later, after learning study design, you can judge whether stronger wording is justified.

Source Note
claims claim-observation-explanation, claim-empirical-question, claim-bounded-question, claim-question-claim-boundary, claim-description-not-causationsources source-nrc-framework

The source supports moving from observed patterns to empirically answerable questions and connecting those questions to specified records and measurements. The clinic record, transfer prompts, and drafting chain are original and do not report real patient data.

aiAssistance.disclosure: VSBDev (github:VSBDev) is accountable for this lesson. Material authoring assistance used Codex by OpenAI, model GPT-5, in the isolated run /root/prem_02_01_author on 2026-07-20. The instructions digest covers the exact UTF-8 task payload supplied by the root agent, including its terminal newline; it excludes hidden provider and system instructions.

items.prompt: A student writes: (1) 'After 15 minutes, the covered cup was 4 °C warmer than the uncovered cup.' (2) 'The cover trapped heat in the cup.' Which option labels the statements correctly?

options.text: Statement 1 is an observation; statement 2 is an explanation.
Statement 1 is an explanation; statement 2 is an observation.

answer.summary: The temperature difference is a record; the heat-trapping statement proposes why it occurred.
answer.reasoning: Statement 1 names the compared cups, a measured temperature difference, and a time. Another person could inspect the record. Statement 2 introduces a mechanism that was not measured directly. The mechanism may later be supported, but it is an explanation rather than the observation itself. If you selected an option saying both are observations, look for the unseen causal action in 'trapped heat.' On a retry, classify this pair: 'The metal strip lengthened by 2 millimetres when warmed' and 'Heating increased particle motion and expanded the strip.'
acceptableResponses: The selected option identifying statement 1 as the observation and statement 2 as the explanation.
commonErrors: Treating a plausible causal sentence as if it were directly observed
Treating every sentence containing a number as an explanation rather than a record

items.prompt: A student notices that bean seedlings on a classroom windowsill appear taller than seedlings on a back shelf after ten days. Write one bounded empirical question from this observation. Make the comparison, record, sample or setting, and time window explicit, and do not assume that light caused the difference.

answer.correct: A complete response asks a neutral question such as: 'For the bean seedlings in this classroom during the next ten days, what is the difference in stem height measured in millimetres between seedlings placed on the windowsill and seedlings placed on the back shelf?' It explicitly names the comparison, observable record, bounded sample or setting, and time window without assuming a cause.
answer.summary: A testable question connects a defined case or contrast to a record within stated boundaries and leaves the answer open.
answer.reasoning: Score one point for each visible feature: (1) windowsill and back-shelf seedlings are both named; (2) the record is operational enough to collect, such as stem height in millimetres; (3) the classroom seedlings and ten-day window are bounded; and (4) the wording asks what the difference is rather than claiming that light caused growth. All four features are required for 4/4. If one is missing, repair only that part and retry with the alternate observation: ice on a shaded pavement patch remained after ice on a sunny patch had melted.
acceptableResponses: Any neutral empirical question that names the two locations, a defined observable growth record, the classroom seedling sample, and a time window.
A one-location time-series question is acceptable if it names the seedling sample, repeated height record, setting, and time window and does not claim a cause.
commonErrors: Asking why light makes the plants grow without first establishing the recordable pattern
Using 'growth' without saying what will be recorded
Omitting the seedlings, setting, or time window
Writing the expected answer into the question

items.prompt: A city bird count found more sparrows near outdoor cafés than in two nearby parks. Someone asks, 'Do cafés cause sparrow problems?' Identify two reasons that question is not empirically answerable as written, rewrite it as one bounded observational question, and name one possible result that would differ from the original expectation.

answer.correct: A complete answer identifies at least two defects, such as the undefined judgment 'problems,' an unsupported causal assumption, an unspecified bird-count record, or missing place and time boundaries. It then asks a neutral bounded question, for example: 'During 08:00–10:00 on five dry weekdays in May, what is the difference in the number of sparrows counted during ten-minute observations at the three named outdoor cafés and the two named parks?' It also gives a possible contrary or different result, such as similar counts or higher park counts.
answer.summary: Replacing judgment and assumed cause with a defined count, comparison, place, and time produces an answerable observational question.
answer.reasoning: Score one point for each criterion: (1) at least two concrete defects are identified; (2) the rewrite names the café and park comparison; (3) it names a sparrow-count record plus place and time boundaries without asserting cause; and (4) it states a result that differs from the original expectation. All four are required for 4/4. If your rewrite still uses 'problems' or 'cause,' replace the judgment with a record and make the wording descriptive. On a retry, use this case: more bicycles were parked near one library entrance than another and someone asks whether the first entrance causes better study habits.
acceptableResponses: Any bounded, neutral observational question with a defined sparrow record, named café and park cases, a stated observation period, and a genuinely different possible result.
commonErrors: Keeping the undefined value judgment 'sparrow problems'
Treating an observed location difference as proof of cause
Naming no count or observation procedure
Giving only the expected result instead of a result that could challenge it

criteria.description: Award 1 point only when the measured temperature difference is identified as the observation; award 0 if it is labelled an explanation or if no choice is made.
Award 1 point only when the heat-trapping statement is identified as the explanation; award 0 if it is treated as directly observed.
Award 1 point only when both windowsill and back-shelf seedlings, or a defensible single-case alternative, are explicit.
Award 1 point only when the response names a collectable record such as stem height in stated units rather than undefined growth.
Award 1 point only when the classroom seedling sample or equivalent setting and a time window are explicit.
Award 1 point only when the wording leaves the result open and does not assert that light or location caused the difference.
Award 1 point only when at least two specific defects are identified, such as undefined judgment, assumed cause, missing record, or missing boundaries.
Award 1 point only when the rewritten question identifies the café and park cases or a comparably explicit set of locations.
Award 1 point only when a sparrow-count record and clear place and time boundaries are included without a causal assertion.
Award 1 point only when a possible result that differs from the original expectation is stated, such as equal counts or higher park counts.

sources.title: A Framework for K-12 Science Education: Practices, Crosscutting Concepts, and Core Ideas
sources.locator: Chapter 3, especially pp. 43–61: scientific practices, Asking Questions and Defining Problems, and Planning and Carrying Out Investigations
sources.license: Copyright © 2012 National Academy of Sciences; all rights reserved. Used only as a factual and standards reference; no source expression, figure, table, or example is reused.

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: The lesson independently synthesizes the report's standards-level propositions about observations, explanations, empirically answerable questions, records, measurements, scope, and evidence. Its apple, sugar, pond, clinic, seedling, cup, and bird cases; question builder; assessment; and prose are original and do not reproduce the report's wording, structure, examples, figures, or tables.

agentAccess.notes: Before substantive access, the public National Academies terms route was inspected. No term prohibiting this limited agent reading was found. The chapter was then read through the public HTTPS read-online route without login, download, bypass, or automated bulk collection. This is a dated observation, not a warranty from the publisher.

claims.statement: A scientific observation or data record must be kept distinct from the inference or explanation built from it, even when the explanation is plausible.
claims.scope: Introductory scientific reasoning across observational and experimental contexts.
claims.uncertainty: What counts as an adequate record and how inference is warranted depend on the discipline, instrument, and study design; those later judgments are outside this lesson.

claims.statement: A scientific question can arise from an observed feature, pattern, or contradiction and is empirically answerable when observations or measurements can bear on its answer.
claims.scope: Question formulation for introductory science; meaningful ethical, practical, and value questions may require evidence without being settled by evidence alone.

claims.statement: A question becomes practically investigable when it identifies the case or comparison, the observation or measurement that would answer it, and boundaries consistent with available scope and resources.
claims.scope: A drafting heuristic for bounded introductory questions, not a universal grammatical template or a complete study-design rule.
claims.uncertainty: Different scientific fields operationalize cases, comparisons, measurements, and feasible scope differently.

claims.statement: Questions are empirically answerable rather than true or false; hypotheses, predictions, explanations, and claims are the statements that observations can support, challenge, or contradict.
claims.scope: Introductory distinction between question formulation and evidence-bearing propositions; detailed philosophy of falsifiability is outside scope.
claims.uncertainty: Terminology varies across disciplines, and researchers sometimes use 'testable question' informally without making this logical distinction explicit.

claims.statement: An observed difference or association can motivate a question but does not by itself establish that one factor caused the other.
claims.scope: Introductory boundary on conclusions drawn from observed comparisons and associations; detailed causal-inference criteria are reserved for later lessons.

claims.statement: Scientific practice uses multiple interacting methods rather than one universal linear procedure followed in the same order across all fields.
claims.scope: A boundary against presenting a single classroom sequence as the universal scientific method.
claims.uncertainty: Individual investigations may still use systematic ordered procedures appropriate to their question and field.

terms.definition: A record of what was noticed or measured, stated so that its object, conditions, and result can be checked.
A conclusion drawn from observations and reasoning rather than something recorded directly.
Answerable or assessable using observations or measurements from the world.
A question connected to observations or measurements that could produce more than one possible answer within stated boundaries.
A stated limit on the sample, setting, time, scale, or conditions to which a question refers.
A proposed explanation or relationship that leads to expectations evidence can test; developed in the next lesson.

attributionHtml:
Attribution

Lesson content

From an observation to a testable question, version 0.1.0, is original course content contributed by VSBDev with disclosed Codex assistance. It is licensed under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0).

Suggested attribution: “From an observation to a testable question” by VSBDev for EmbeddedKnowledge, version 0.1.0, CC BY 4.0.

Sources and reuse

The National Research Council consensus report listed as source-nrc-framework was used only to verify standards-level facts about scientific observations, explanations, questions, evidence, records, measurements, and feasible scope. No wording, example, figure, table, dataset, or other protected expression from that source is reused or adapted. All lesson cases, values, prose, prompts, question-builder structure, feedback, and assessment items were independently created for this lesson.

The apple, sugar, pond, campus-clinic, seedling, cup, and sparrow cases are original fictional teaching cases. They do not report real experiments, patients, students, or datasets.

No third-party assets or datasets are included.

Agent disclosure

VSBDev (github:VSBDev) is the accountable principal. Material authoring assistance used Codex by OpenAI, model GPT-5, in isolated run /root/prem_02_01_author on 2026-07-20. The recorded SHA-256 digest covers the exact discloseable UTF-8 task payload supplied by the root agent, including its terminal newline; it does not cover hidden provider or system instructions.
